does: This paper introduces AIR-Bench 2024, a comprehensive AI safety benchmark containing 5,694 diverse prompts organized across 314 granular risk categories derived from actual government regulations and company policies. The authors systematically analyze 8 government regulations and 16 company policies to construct a four-tiered safety taxonomy, then curate and manually audit benchmark prompts to reflect real-world safety concerns rather than literature-driven intuitions. The paper evaluates leading language models against this benchmark, revealing how well current foundation models align with regulatory and policy requirements for safe deployment.
connects: This work builds on the growing ecosystem of AI safety benchmarks like BBQ, ToxiGen, and others that assess harmful content generation, but addresses a key limitation: most existing benchmarks derive safety categories from academic literature or common-sense reasoning rather than actual regulatory frameworks. The paper directly responds to the fragmentation problem where different benchmarks use incompatible category systems, making cross-benchmark evaluation difficult. It also extends the emerging practice of regulation-informed AI governance, connecting to prior work on policy-aligned evaluation and the broader movement toward empirical grounding of safety standards in actual government and corporate requirements rather than researcher intuitions.
enables: This benchmark enables more systematic evaluation of foundation models against real-world compliance requirements, supporting regulatory compliance assessment and policy-aligned model development. It creates a standardized reference for comparing how well models meet specific government and company safety requirements, facilitating better-informed deployment decisions. The regulation-grounded taxonomy established here could guide future safety research by identifying previously overlooked risk categories and enabling targeted safety improvement. The benchmark also supports developing safety techniques that directly address regulatory concerns, and may inform regulators by demonstrating which compliance requirements are most challenging for current models, pointing toward areas requiring stronger governance or technical mitigations.
